intro.title: The King's Spring Progress
intro.body: Hittite kings spent the spring touring sanctuaries before campaigning. The AN.TAH.SUM festival route reconstructed from cuneiform tablets links Hattusa with the great cult-centres of central Anatolia — Nerik, Zippalanda, Tahurpa, Arinna — over roughly 38 days.

stops.name: Hattusa
stops.note: Capital and start of the AN.TAH.SUM spring festival route. Royal residence on the central Anatolian plateau.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Cyclopean basalt walls of an upland fortress, lion-gate, chariots assembling in the lower town.

stops.name: Tahurpa
stops.note: First festival stop south-west of Hattusa, mentioned in CTH 604. Exact site uncertain; conventionally placed near Yörüklü.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Village shrine on a low knoll, royal chariot arriving with retinue on foot.

stops.name: Arinna
stops.note: Cult-centre of the Sun-Goddess of Arinna, chief deity of the Hittite pantheon. Identified by many with modern Alaca Höyük.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Sun-disk standard above a stone temple precinct, priestesses leading procession.

stops.name: Zippalanda
stops.note: Cult-centre of the Storm-God of Zippalanda. Convincingly identified with Uşaklı Höyük on the basis of recent excavation.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Mountain shrine with bull-statue, storm clouds gathering above.

stops.name: Nerik
stops.note: Northern cult-centre of the Storm-God of Nerik, lost to the Kaska and recovered under Hattusili III. Identified with Oymaağaç Höyük.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Hilltop temple in pine country, priests with libation vessels, distant smoke of frontier watchfires.
